_metadata.purpose: Authoritative POI knowledge base for the AtoC Korea catalog — 82 verified-fact entries with visitBasics + convenience + smartNotes + _poi_meta. Surfaced into 30 tour itinerary stops at the v17 schema with per-stop kb_version tracking. Two metadata surfaces (`_metadata` legacy + `_kb_metadata` modern) version-consistent since Batch 14a (v1.18) reconciliation. v1.21 introduces the compound-stop pattern (Jusangjeolli+Cheonjeyeon) with no new POI additions.

field_format._poi_meta_extras: additional verified facts to merge into _poi_meta

sources_consulted: Jeju King Cherry Blossom Festival (19th edition)
UNESCO World Heritage Centre (Bulguksa, Sansa, Gyeongju Historic Areas)
Cultural Heritage Administration of Korea (NT 20, NT 21, NT 22, NT 23, NT 26, NT 27, NT 29, NT 31, NT 188, NT 290, Folklore Material No. 27, Intangible No. 86-3)
MCST '100 Best Tourist Attractions in Korea 2025–2026' list
American Battle Monuments Commission (Korean War Memorial)

changelog.notes: Batch 13a (Busan cruise pair: charter + small-group) reuses 5 existing v1.15 POIs. No new keys added. Verified deep-research facts written into per-stop description bodies in tour JSONs: (1) Yongdusan Park rebranded as Busan Diamond Tower in 2021 after redevelopment, tower top modeled after Bulguksa Dabotap baldaquin, (2) Taejongdae's Yeongdo Lighthouse built 1906 (Busan's oldest, 10th in Korea) + modern successor 2004, wave-cut terrace 120,000 years old, (3) UN Memorial Cemetery donated to UN by Korea in 1959, Wall of Remembrance 2006 = 40,896 names on 140 marble panels, 'Turn Toward Busan' Nov 11 11:00 AM, (4) Busan Port International Passenger Terminal opened Aug 31, 2015 = Asia's 2nd largest after Shanghai, 2.78M annual passenger capacity, (5) Citizens' Bell at Yongdusan struck 33 times on New Year's Eve and March 1.

visitBasics.hours: Daily 04:30–19:20 (last admission 18:50)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free; private-car parking is paid
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 108 steps through pine grove, multiple seaside terraces and stairs

convenience.restroom: Available near main entrance and inside complex
convenience.parking: Haedong Yonggungsa Parking Lot (paid, per Visit Busan official)

smartNotes.photo: Sunrise rock at the seaward edge; stairs next to Daeungjeon Hall give the classic ocean-and-temple frame
smartNotes.facilities: Souvenir/snack stalls and small cafe at upper plaza; coastal trail leads further along the rocks
smartNotes.tip: Side doors for entering halls (front door reserved for monks); Buddha's Birthday (Apr–May) brings the lantern display

visitBasics.hours: Sky Capsule and Beach Train ~09:30–18:30 (extended to 20:30 in summer); Cheongsapo Daritdol Skywalk free 24h
visitBasics.closed: Operations weather-dependent; check the day of
visitBasics.admission: Free area; Sky Capsule 40,000 KRW (2 ppl) / 45,000 (3) / 50,000 (4) one-way; Beach Train ticketed separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy along the wooden Green Railway boardwalk (Mipo↔Cheongsapo 2.3 km, ~30 min); flat at stations

convenience.restroom: Available at Mipo, Cheongsapo and Songjeong stations
convenience.parking: Public lots near Mipo Station and Cheongsapo Station (paid)

cheongsapo_blue_line_park.photo_spot_pin: Cheongsapo Station crosswalk (Slam Dunk look-alike) and Daritdol Skywalk glass deck

smartNotes.photo: Cheongsapo Station crosswalk for the famous Slam Dunk-style frame; Daritdol Skywalk for glass-bottom sea view
smartNotes.facilities: Ticketing counters at Mipo, Cheongsapo and Songjeong only; convenience store and food at Mipo plaza
smartNotes.tip: Sky Capsule books out fast at sunset slots — reserve in advance via Klook or the official site; Mipo→Cheongsapo direction has the better light at sunset

visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat grass paths and paved walkways across 14 ha of grounds

convenience.restroom: Available at Memorial Service Hall and visitor center
convenience.parking: On-site lot — free for first 90 min, then ₩1,200/hr

smartNotes.photo: Wall of Remembrance (40,896 names on 140 marble panels) and the Symbolic Area where the 22 sending-state flags fly
smartNotes.facilities: Memorial Service Hall, Memorabilia Hall, UN Forces Monument, and adjacent UN Peace Memorial Hall
smartNotes.tip: Watch the 12-minute documentary in Memorial Service Hall first (English available); dress neatly, keep voices low, and no food on the grounds

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (Mar–Oct) / 09:00–17:00 (Nov–Feb); some shops/cafes keep their own hours
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (a living residential community)
visitBasics.admission: Free village entry; optional Stamp Map ≈ 2,000 KRW
visitBasics.walking: Strenuous — very steep alleys and stairs; not stroller- or wheelchair-friendly in many sections

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the Tourist Information Center and around the main viewpoints
convenience.parking: Gamcheon Public Parking Lot 2 (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Little Prince and Fox bench (long queue) and Haneul Maru Observatory for the sweeping rainbow-roof and bay view
smartNotes.facilities: Hanbok rental, craft workshops, Korean candy stalls, cafes; small village museum at the entrance
smartNotes.tip: Residents live here — keep voices down, no drones, take rubbish with you; wear grippy shoes for the steep lanes

visitBasics.hours: Daily 05:00–22:00 (vendor hours vary; live-fish section officially 06:00–22:00)
visitBasics.closed: 1st and 3rd Tuesdays of every month
visitBasics.admission: Free entry; pay-as-you-go for seafood and 2F cooking-fee
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — ground floor flat (wet, can be slippery); stairs to 2F sashimi/restaurant level

convenience.restroom: Available inside the main building on each floor
convenience.parking: Basement lot at the main building, ₩700 / 10 min

smartNotes.photo: Outdoor pier behind the building for the harbor-and-fishing-boat shot; Jagalchi ajummas at the live-fish stalls
smartNotes.facilities: 1F live seafood, 2F sashimi & restaurants, dried-fish section, rooftop Sky Park (when open), Sashimi Center buffet on 5F
smartNotes.tip: Buy the fish on 1F, then bring it upstairs to a 2F restaurant and pay a small cooking fee — that's the local way; address 52 Jagalchihaean-ro, Jung-gu

visitBasics.hours: Park 04:00–24:00 (Mar–Oct) / 05:00–24:00 (Nov–Feb); facilities close ~20:00
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; Danubi Train and lighthouse close in inclement weather
visitBasics.admission: Park free; Danubi Train 4,000 KRW round-trip / 2,000 one-way (adult), 2,000/1,500 youth/child
visitBasics.walking: Strenuous if walking the full 4.3 km loop; lighthouse area has many steep stairs (no elevator)

convenience.restroom: Available at the main entrance, Observatory, and lighthouse plaza
convenience.parking: Taejongdae Park Parking Lot (paid; vehicles enter from 09:00)

smartNotes.photo: Yeongdo Lighthouse cliff edge and the Observatory overlook toward Oryukdo and (on clear days) Tsushima
smartNotes.facilities: Tourist information center at the entrance, Danubi Train ticket booth, food/snack stalls at the rocks below the lighthouse
smartNotes.tip: Buy Danubi Train tickets in advance during high season — your ticket is tied to a specific time slot; use the Visit Busan City Tour Bus wristband for 25% off the train fare

visitBasics.hours: Park 24h year-round; Busan Diamond Tower observatory 10:00–22:00 (last admission 21:30)
visitBasics.closed: Park open year-round; Tower may close in severe weather
visitBasics.admission: Park free; Busan Diamond Tower 12,000 KRW (adult) / 9,000 KRW (senior 65+ / child 3–12) / under-3 free
visitBasics.walking: Easy — escalator from Gwangbok-ro / Nampo-dong handles the climb; gentle paths within the park

convenience.restroom: Available within the park and inside the Tower lobby
convenience.parking: No dedicated lot at the park; nearby Lotte Department Store Gwangbok lot (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Tower observatory windows facing Busan Port at golden hour; flower clock and Statue of Admiral Yi Sun-sin in the park
smartNotes.facilities: Souvenir shops, cafe, AR/media zones inside the Tower; bell pavilion and dragon sculpture in the park
smartNotes.tip: Climb via the outdoor escalator from Gwangbok-ro Fashion Street rather than the stairs; Tower pairs well with Nampo-dong dinner — visit at sunset for day-and-night views

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (last entry 17:00)
visitBasics.closed: Mondays (some sources); open during major Korean holidays
visitBasics.admission: Adults 8,000 KRW / Under 18 5,000 KRW (no senior or group discount — private property)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — gentle slopes, mostly flat, ~1–2 hour loop on natural unpaved trails

convenience.restroom: Available near the entrance/ticket booth
convenience.parking: Free spacious lot at the entrance (closes after 18:00)

smartNotes.photo: Gutteo Bamboo Grove ('The King: Eternal Monarch' filming site) and the second bamboo forest with sunlight filtering through the canopy
smartNotes.facilities: Free bamboo walking-stick rental and mosquito spray at the gate; no on-site cafe — bring water
smartNotes.tip: Pets, drones, alcohol, smoking, bicycles and music speakers are all prohibited (working forest with wildlife); plant/insect collection is forbidden

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (last admission 17:00)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free (since 2023, per the revised Cultural Heritage Protection Act)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — stone steps, slopes and gravel paths; comfortable shoes recommended

convenience.restroom: Free public restrooms (designed in mini-temple style) inside the complex
convenience.parking: On-site lot (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Cheongungyo / Baegungyo bridge stairway (NT 23) below Jahamun Gate; Dabotap (NT 20) and Seokgatap (NT 21) pagodas in the main courtyard
smartNotes.facilities: Tourist Information Center at the entrance; Bulguksa Museum (separate ticket 2,000 / 1,000 KRW, closed Mondays)
smartNotes.tip: Modest dress; remove shoes when entering the halls; no drones; do not photograph monks or worshippers (signs marked clearly)

visitBasics.hours: Weekdays 10:00–18:00 / Weekends & holidays 10:00–19:00 (last admission 30 min before close); extended Saturday & last-Wednesday hours seasonally
visitBasics.closed: New Year's Day, Seollal, Chuseok, first Monday of March and November
visitBasics.admission: Free (special exhibitions may require advance reservation)
visitBasics.walking: Easy — multiple flat galleries plus a large outdoor exhibition area

convenience.restroom: Available in every wing and outdoor exhibit area
convenience.parking: Free on-site lot

smartNotes.photo: Sacred Bell of King Seongdeok (NT 29) at the front of the museum; outdoor stone-pagoda field at sunset
smartNotes.facilities: Silla History Gallery, Silla Art Gallery, Wolji Gallery (Anapji Pond relics), Children's Museum, English audio guide, museum shop, cafe
smartNotes.tip: Free English museum tour on Sundays at 10:00 and 14:00 (90 min, departs from 1F Silla History Gallery); the Cheonma-zuka Golden Crown (NT 188) is the must-see in the Silla History Gallery

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor village 24h; shops and the Choi Family House typically 10:00–17:00 (some take a 15:00–18:00 lunch break)
visitBasics.closed: Village open year-round; Choi Family House may close on Mondays
visitBasics.admission: Free village entry; some workshops and the Gyeongju Choi Wealthy Family House may charge a small fee
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat, stone-paved lanes; comfortable shoes recommended

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms near the village entrance and main plaza
convenience.parking: On-site lot near the village (free, fills quickly on weekends)

smartNotes.photo: Tiled-roof rooflines along the stone-walled lanes and the Choi Family House courtyard
smartNotes.facilities: Hanbok rental, Gyodong Beopju traditional liquor shop (Important Intangible Cultural Property No. 86-3), local eateries selling Gyochon makgeolli, ssambap and injeolmi ice cream
smartNotes.tip: Pair with Woljeonggyo Bridge across the stream; weekday visits are noticeably quieter than weekends, and many shops close around 17:00

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor bridge 24h; pavilion exhibition hall typically 09:00–18:00
visitBasics.closed: Bridge open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat, wide wooden bridge deck; pavilion stairs at each end

convenience.restroom: Public restroom on the Gyochon side of the bridge
convenience.parking: Use Gyochon Hanok Village parking (a 2-min walk away)

smartNotes.photo: Reflection of the illuminated bridge on Namcheon Stream after sunset; the pavilion's traditional dancheong at golden hour
smartNotes.facilities: Inside one pavilion is a small free exhibition on the bridge's history and Silla-era engineering
smartNotes.tip: Reconstructed in 2018 from records of the original 760 AD Silla bridge — best visited at dusk to catch both the daylight architecture and the night illumination (lit ~sunset to about 22:00)

visitBasics.hours: Mar–May & Sep–Oct: 09:00–18:00; Jun–Aug: 09:00–18:30; Nov–Feb: 09:00–17:00 (last admission 1 hour before close)
visitBasics.closed: Tuesdays
visitBasics.admission: Adult 3,000 KRW / Youth (7–18) 1,500 KRW; free for ≤6, seniors 65+, hanbok wearers
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — large palace grounds (1–2 hours of walking); paths are gravel and stone

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms throughout the palace grounds
convenience.parking: Limited on-site paid lot; nearby public lots near Gwanghwamun

smartNotes.photo: Royal Guard Changing Ceremony at Gwanghwamun (10:00 and 14:00, ~20 min, free; cancelled on Tuesdays and in heavy rain); Gyeonghoeru Pavilion reflected in its pond
smartNotes.facilities: Free guided tours in English (11:00, 13:30, 15:30); hanbok rental shops surround the palace gates; National Folk Museum and Children's Museum on the grounds
smartNotes.tip: Wearing hanbok gets you in for free — many rental shops offer a 1–4 hour package; arrive 10–15 min before the 10:00 ceremony for a clear view

visitBasics.hours: Open 24 hours (outdoor); resident-quiet hours 22:00–10:00 enforced
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (residential — please be respectful)
visitBasics.admission: Free; some hanok museums charge separately (≈ 3,000 KRW)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — uphill cobblestone alleys; not stroller-friendly in places

convenience.restroom: Bukchon Cultural Center and Bukchon Tourist Information Center provide public restrooms
convenience.parking: No dedicated lot — use Anguk Station-area paid lots and walk in

smartNotes.photo: Gahoe-dong Alley 8 (downhill view toward N Seoul Tower over hanok roofs) and Alley 31 — the two iconic 'Bukchon 8 Views' angles
smartNotes.facilities: Bukchon Cultural Center (free programs), small hanok cafes and craft workshops; map and route info at the visitor center
smartNotes.tip: This is a residential neighborhood — keep voices down, do not enter private gates, and respect the marshals on Gahoe-dong Alley 11; weekday mornings are quietest

visitBasics.hours: Food alley daily 09:00–23:00 (most stalls 11:00 onward); general market 09:00–18:00 (closed Sun); apparel 10:00–19:00 (closed Sun)
visitBasics.closed: Many food stalls close on Sundays or Mondays — varies by stall
visitBasics.admission: Free entry; per-meal budget ≈ 8,000–15,000 KRW
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat covered alleys; can be very crowded at lunch and dinner

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at market entrances
convenience.parking: Use Jongno 4-ga Jongmyo Public Parking Lot (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Bindae-tteok (mung bean pancake) stalls being fried on cast-iron griddles; Mayak Gimbap rolls at Mother & Daughter's Gimbap
smartNotes.facilities: Hundreds of food stalls, sit-down restaurants on the outer ring, hanbok and vintage clothing on the 2F
smartNotes.tip: Cash is still preferred at smaller stalls; lunch rush 12:00–14:00 means long queues — try arriving 11:00 or after 14:30; Netflix-famous knife-cut noodles at Cho Yonsoon's stall

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor street 24h; shops typically 10:00–22:00; pedestrian-only on Saturdays 14:00–22:00 and Sundays 10:00–22:00
visitBasics.closed: Most shops open year-round; some close on Mondays
visitBasics.admission: Free; some museums and craft workshops charge separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — wide, mostly flat, pedestrian-friendly main street with branching alleys

convenience.restroom: Available inside Anyoung Insadong, Ssamziegil, and at Anguk Station Exit 6
convenience.parking: Use nearby paid lots (Insa-dong is largely pedestrian); Anguk Station has metered street parking

smartNotes.photo: Ssamziegil's spiraling indoor courtyard and the calligraphy-style Hangul shopfronts along Insadong-gil
smartNotes.facilities: Roughly 40% of Korea's antique shops, plus traditional tea houses, hanji paper shops, calligraphy stores, art galleries, and the Korean Craft and Design Foundation Gallery
smartNotes.tip: Branch into the side alleys for the quieter tea houses and locally-run craft shops — the main street is the touristy part; weekend afternoons are pedestrian-only and most lively but also the most crowded

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor 24h year-round; cherry blossom illumination ~sunset to 22:00 in early April
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; some lakeside cafes close Mondays
visitBasics.admission: Free (lake and walking trail); paddle boats and bike rentals priced separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat 8km paved loop trail along the lakeside; can be sampled in 1–2 km segments without doing the full loop

convenience.restroom: Available at the Tourist Information Center, Bomun Pavilion, and major resort access points along the loop
convenience.parking: Multiple free public lots around the resort complex (Hoban Square, Bomun Pavilion); fills quickly during the festival weekend

smartNotes.photo: Bomun Pavilion (CNN '50 most beautiful places in Korea') and the wooden bridge for reflection shots; sunset around the east shore near Gyeongju World
smartNotes.facilities: Bicycle and tandem rentals (~₩10,000/3hr), paddle boats, lakeside cafes and restaurants, Bomun Open Air Stage with free traditional music performances Apr–Jan
smartNotes.tip: Cherry blossom peak is typically late March to early April (~7-day window); arrive on a weekday morning or after 16:00 to avoid the worst weekend crowds — full loop walk takes ~2 hours, but most tours sample only 1–2 km

visitBasics.hours: Park 09:00–22:00 (until 21:00 in winter); Cheonmachong Tomb interior 09:00–22:00
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Park: Free. Cheonmachong Tomb interior (NT-class artifacts): Adult 3,000 KRW / Teenager 2,000 / Child 1,000
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat grassy paths between the burial mounds; spread across 126,500 m² (good for stroller and senior visitors)

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the main entrance and near Cheonmachong Tomb
convenience.parking: Daereungwon parking lot at the main gate (paid)

smartNotes.photo: Daereungwon Stone Wall Road (cherry blossom tunnel — annual festival Apr 3–5); the giant magnolia tree behind Hwangnamdaechong; Tomb of King Michu surrounded by cherry trees
smartNotes.facilities: Small museum inside Cheonmachong Tomb showing 11,526 excavated artifacts including the famous Heavenly Horse painting (the only known Silla-era painting); Day Pass option available (₩4,000–8,000 covering Daereungwon + Donggung & Wolji Pond)
smartNotes.tip: Climbing the burial mounds is prohibited (heavy fine); 30–60 min is typical for a walk-through, longer if you enter Cheonmachong; pairs naturally with Cheomseongdae and Woljeonggyo Bridge — all within a 3 km radius

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor viewing 24h; cherry blossom illumination ~sunset to 22:00 in early April
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (it is a stone monument in a public field — no admission gate)
visitBasics.admission: Free
visitBasics.walking: Very easy — flat open field; closest stop on the Gyeongju downtown loop

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the adjacent visitor center / Donggung area
convenience.parking: Cheomseongdae parking lot (paid) and shared parking with the wider Gyeongju Historic Area

smartNotes.photo: Cheomseongdae itself with cherry blossoms or canola flowers in the surrounding field; the Gyerim Forest immediately south is also photogenic
smartNotes.facilities: Open field with seasonal flower plantings (cherry blossoms in spring, cosmos in autumn); food trucks during festivals; English info plaques at base of the tower
smartNotes.tip: The 27-tier stone tower from 647 AD is the oldest astronomical observatory in East Asia and a National Treasure — counting the stones (365 to match days of the year) is a fun activity for kids; only 5–15 min needed for the stop itself

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor temple grounds 24h; Seongbo Museum 09:00–17:00; Daeungjeon main hall typically open daylight hours
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; Seongbo Museum closed Mondays
visitBasics.admission: Temple grounds: Free (most general visit areas); Seongbo Museum (first Buddhist museum in Korea, 600+ paintings, 30,000 artifacts): typically ₩2,000–3,000 adult — confirm at entrance
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 1.2 km riverside paved trail from parking lot to main temple entrance (flat, can go barefoot per VisitKorea), then temple courtyards spread across hillside slopes (some stairs and gentle inclines)

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the parking lot, Seongbo Museum entrance, and inside the main temple compound near Daeungjeon
convenience.parking: Two large paid parking lots near the trailhead (Yangsan City + Tongdosa joint operation); fills during festivals (plum blossom, Buddha's Birthday lantern festival)

smartNotes.photo: Jajangmae (지장매) — the 350-year-old red plum tree planted by Master Jajang, peak ~Late February to Early March, 'first messenger of spring'; Daeungjeon (National Treasure #290 — unique because it has no Buddha image inside, instead the Diamond Stairway/Geumgang Gyedan enshrines actual Buddha relics behind it); the riverside pine forest trail (curved 'dragon-branch' red pines, Iljumun gate approach)
smartNotes.facilities: Templestay program available in English (lotus lanterns, tea ceremony, meditation, 108 prostrations); Seongbo Museum (largest Buddhist museum in Korea); 17 hermitages on the mountain (most require additional hiking — not part of standard temple visit)
smartNotes.tip: UNESCO World Heritage 'Sansa, Buddhist Mountain Monasteries in Korea' (2018) — one of Korea's Three Jewel Temples (Bulbo Temple, enshrining genuine Buddha relics). Daeungjeon famously contains NO Buddha image — only an altar facing the Diamond Stairway. Best visit time is early morning before tour buses arrive (08:30–10:00) or late afternoon (15:00–16:30) for golden light on the temple

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor village/festival grounds: open 24h during bloom (~late Feb to mid Mar); festival main events typically Fri–Sun in early March
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (the village itself); plum blossom peak window only ~10 days mid-March
visitBasics.admission: Free — the village and trails are open to the public; festival booth purchases are pay-direct
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat farm paths along the Nakdong River + railway tracks; can be sampled in 1–2 km segments without committing to a long walk

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at Wondong Station (railway), the festival main stage area, and the visitor center during festival weekends
convenience.parking: Free public lots near Wondong Station; fills heavily on festival weekend mornings (insider tip: arrive before 09:00 or after 15:00)

smartNotes.photo: The signature shot is plum blossom branches framing the Nakdong River + the railway tracks — passing trains create a once-per-30-minutes photo moment; festival weekends add traditional music busking under the trees
smartNotes.facilities: Wondong Plum Blossom Festival (typically March 1–3 + 10-day extended bloom window); 'Plum Noodle Market' selling locally made plum-infused noodles, plum extracts, plum highball, Maesil products; 'Minari Festival' running concurrently with greenhouse pop-up restaurants serving Minari Samgyeopsal — the area's signature spring dish (clean-water parsley grilled with pork belly, only available at this season)
smartNotes.tip: Yangsan Wondong is 'romantic and nostalgic' (Korean tourism tagline) compared to Gwangyang's grand mountain-scale plum festival — smaller, less-crowded, with a stronger railway/river aesthetic. Pairs naturally with Tongdosa Temple (~25 min drive) for a Yangsan-focused half day.

_poi_meta.historical_feature: Tochika — 1945 Imperial Japanese military pillbox/communication post at summit

visitBasics.hours: Trail accessible roughly 05:00–18:00 (varies seasonally; longer in summer May–Aug)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; closures possible in extreme weather
visitBasics.admission: Free; no entrance fee, no reservation required (only Seongpanak/Gwaneumsa summit trails require advance QR reservation)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 1.3 km one-way, ~200 m elevation gain via wooden stairs and boardwalks; 30–40 min up, plan 1–1.5 hr round trip

convenience.restroom: Available at Eorimok Visitor Center near the trailhead
convenience.parking: Eorimok Trail parking lot at the visitor center (free)

smartNotes.photo: Summit observation deck for 360° view of Jeju City, the northern coast and the higher Hallasan peaks (Baengnokdam crater on clear days). Tochika ruins for a quick history shot
smartNotes.facilities: Visitor Center with information panels and restrooms at trailhead; benches and rest points along the trail; no shops or vending past the entrance
smartNotes.tip: Wooden stairs are slippery after rain and icy in winter (Dec–Feb) — micro-spikes recommended in winter. Pets and drones are not allowed in Hallasan National Park

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (last admission ~17:00; hours may shift seasonally with sunset)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Adults ₩2,000 / Youth (7–24) ₩1,000 / Free for under 7 and over 65; group rate (10+) ₩1,600 adult / ₩600 youth
visitBasics.walking: Easy — 0.8 km loop on paved walkways and wooden viewing decks; ~12–15 min walk, fully flat

convenience.restroom: Available at the entrance plaza and visitor area
convenience.parking: Paid parking lot adjacent to entrance — ₩1,000 small / ₩2,000 medium (sedan to 15-seater) / ₩3,000 large vehicles

smartNotes.photo: First viewing deck for the classic hexagonal-columns frame; far deck for waves crashing into the columns (best at high tide — waves can shoot 20+ m up the cliff)
smartNotes.facilities: Souvenir kiosks at entrance selling Hallabong (Jeju tangerine) juice in dol-hareubang shaped bottles, hot dogs, octopus skewers, ice cream and accessories; ICC Jeju (Convention Center) and Yakcheonsa Temple are walking distance
smartNotes.tip: Plan ~30–45 min total. The wooden deck doesn't allow descent to the columns themselves — they are viewed from above. Best photos when sunlight hits at an angle (mid-morning or late afternoon); spray reaches the deck at high tide so cameras may need protection

_poi_meta.name_meaning: Pond of the Emperor of Heaven

visitBasics.hours: Mar–Oct 08:00–18:30; Nov–Feb 09:00–17:30. Last admission 1 hour before closing
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Adults ₩2,500 / Youth & Children ₩1,350 (covers larger valley and Seonimgyo Bridge)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — many stairs across the three tiers; total ~1–1.5 km loop with significant ascent/descent. Plan 1–1.5 hr

convenience.restroom: Available near the ticket office at the entrance (none past the bridge)
convenience.parking: On-site parking lot — free for visitors

smartNotes.photo: Seonimgyo Bridge (vermilion arch with seven nymphs carved on the trusses) for the classic shot; viewing deck for the second tier — the most active and photogenic waterfall (30 m drop into emerald pool); Cheonjeru Pavilion deck for distant overview
smartNotes.facilities: Benches and shaded rest spots along the path; no shops past the bridge — pack water in summer; Chilseonyeo (Seven Nymphs) Festival held here in May of even-numbered years
smartNotes.tip: First tier ONLY flows after heavy rain (often dry in winter); the second tier is the reliable attraction. Lots of stairs — comfortable shoes required. Note: do NOT confuse with Cheonjiyeon Falls (different waterfall in Seogwipo old town). Swimming prohibited

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–19:00 (Apr–Oct) / 09:00–18:00 (Nov–Mar); subject to change in adverse weather
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free entry to museum and tea fields; tea, desserts, classes priced separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — paved paths and ramps throughout; tea fields fully flat. Stroller- and wheelchair-friendly

convenience.restroom: Available throughout the museum and adjacent Innisfree Jeju House
convenience.parking: Large free parking lot on-site

smartNotes.photo: The teacup-shaped main building exterior; 2nd-floor observatory window over the Seogwang tea fields; the row of giant teacup planters at the field entrance; Innisfree Jeju House garden path with blue flowers in season
smartNotes.facilities: Cafe (matcha ice cream, green tea swiss roll, Hallabong/tangerine tea, Camellia flower tea); flagship gift shop; Tea Stone (since 2013) — paid tea brewing classes; adjacent Innisfree Jeju House (sister Amorepacific brand) with handmade soap workshop (~₩20,000) and Green Cafe brunch
smartNotes.tip: Arrive at opening (09:00) on weekdays — tour buses crowd the place from 10:00 onward. Green tea ice cream queue gets long midday. Pair with Innisfree Jeju House across the path for a 1.5–2 hr visit. Combo well with Sanbangsan (~25 min) or Hallim Park (~30 min)

_poi_meta.promenade_named_after: Jang Han Chul (Jeju-born Joseon-era writer on Korean maritime history)

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor area 24h; cafes typically 09:00–22:00 (vary by venue)
visitBasics.closed: Outdoor area open year-round; individual cafes have their own days off
visitBasics.admission: Free area; food & drinks priced individually (~₩6,000–12,000 per cafe order)
visitBasics.walking: Easy — paved coastal promenade between Gwakji Beach and Handam Village (~2 km, 20–30 min one way)

convenience.restroom: Inside cafes and at small public facilities along the coastal trail
convenience.parking: Limited — small public lots near the main cafes; some venues offer free private parking for patrons. Roads are narrow, parking can be challenging on weekends

smartNotes.photo: West-facing coastal cliffs at sunset (one of the best sunset spots on Jeju); the small cove at Handam for transparent kayaks; iconic Bomnal Cafe terrace seafront; Gwakji Beach 'jelly sea' shallows in summer
smartNotes.facilities: Dozens of cafes, bakeries (Cafe Knotted, Randy's Donuts), seafood ramyeon at Nolman (₩10,000, single dish), burger shops; transparent kayak rentals at Handam (~₩10,000/30 min, 2-person boats with life jackets)
smartNotes.tip: Sunset is the headline — west-facing coast catches the sun dropping into the sea. Weekday afternoons quieter; weekend afternoons can be packed and parking near impossible. Bus 202/100/200 from Jeju City stops at Handam-dong

_poi_meta.unique_feature: Only waterfall in Asia falling directly into the ocean
_poi_meta.legend_inscription: Seobulgwacha — origin of the Seogwipo place-name (Seobul Headed Back West)

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–17:40 (last admission 30 min before closing; slight seasonal variation)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (occasional closures for high tide / typhoon)
visitBasics.admission: Adults ₩2,000 / Youth (7–24) ₩1,000; free under 7 and 65+
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — ~130 stairs down to the base, then unmarked rocky terrain to reach the waterfall. Stairs slippery when wet; rocks slippery and irregular — sturdy shoes essential

convenience.restroom: At the entrance/ticketing area before the descent (none at the base)
convenience.parking: On-site parking lot at the entrance

smartNotes.photo: Mid-descent platform for the full-cliff waterfall + ocean frame; rock-hopping at the base for close-up shots with the ocean spray (be cautious — slippery); the cliff-top observatory for distant view of Seopseom and Munseom Islands
smartNotes.facilities: Souvenir/snack stalls and ramyeon shops near the entrance; Haenyeo (women diver) ajumma stalls at the base selling fresh sea urchin and conch sashimi (seasonal); Sojeongbang Waterfall ~300 m east; Seogwipo Maeil Olle Market 12 min walk
smartNotes.tip: Best flow after heavy rain or in spring (Hallasan snowmelt) — flow can be light in summer dry spells. AVOID flip-flops on the rock terrain. Watch tide — at high tide many rocks at the base are submerged. Best light: mid-morning (10:00) and late afternoon (15:00–17:00)

visitBasics.hours: May–Aug 04:30–20:00; Mar–Apr & Sep–Oct 05:00–19:00; Nov–Feb 06:00–18:00. Last ticketing 1 hr before closing
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; opens 1 hr before sunrise to allow first-light viewing
visitBasics.admission: Adults ₩2,000 / Youth (7–24) ₩1,000; free for under 7 and 65+; group (10+) ₩1,600 adult / ₩800 youth
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 0.8 km one-way trail, ~140 m elevation gain via wooden deck staircase (300–500 steps); summit climb 20–30 min, full round trip 45–60 min. One-way path prevents head-on collisions

convenience.restroom: Available at the entrance and at rest points along the trail
convenience.parking: Large free parking lot at the entrance plaza

smartNotes.photo: Summit deck for the bowl-shaped crater shot; mid-trail viewpoint for the dramatic coastline + Udo Island frame; the 99-rock 'crown' rim seen from the base looking up; haenyeo divers at the south coast cove (Udu Beach side)
smartNotes.facilities: Daily haenyeo (women diver) free-diving demonstrations at 13:30 and 15:00 at the south coast cove (singing performance + live diving for shellfish — they sell their fresh catch sliced as sashimi after the show); food/drink stalls at the entrance; Udo Island ferry from Seongsan Port (~5 min away)
smartNotes.tip: TRIPLE UNESCO designation (World Natural Heritage 2007 + Biosphere Reserve + Global Geopark). For the iconic sunrise, arrive 30 min before sunrise time (gates open 1 hr before sunrise). Wooden staircase but stairs throughout — not stroller- or wheelchair-friendly. Combine with Udo Island ferry (~10 min crossing) or Seopjikoji (~15 min drive south) to make a half-day in east Jeju

_poi_meta.name_en: Jeju Stone Park (Jeju Stone Culture Park)
_poi_meta.free_day: Last Wednesday of every month (Cultural Day)
_poi_meta.central_myth: Seolmundae Halmang (giantess goddess who created Jeju Island)
_poi_meta.courses: 3 walking courses — Course 1 ≈ 1 hour, all three ≈ 2h 40min
_poi_meta.highlight_features: Sky Pond, Stone Museum, Obaek Janggun Gallery, Mother's Room (light-shadow sculpture), Seolmundae Halmang Exhibition Hall, traditional Jeju thatched-roof village

visitBasics.hours: Mar–Oct 09:00–18:00 / Nov–Feb 09:00–17:00; ticket office closes ~16:00
visitBasics.closed: Mondays, New Year's Day, Seollal, Chuseok; if Monday is a public holiday, closed the next business day
visitBasics.admission: Adult ₩5,000 / Youth & military ₩3,500; group (10+) ₩4,000/₩2,800; FREE for under 13 and 65+; FREE for everyone on the last Wednesday of every month (Cultural Day)
visitBasics.walking: Easy to moderate — Course 1 (~1 hr) is a flat ~1.5 km loop; full park (Courses 1–3) is ~5 km over rolling parkland and Gotjawal forest. Mostly paved, some forest paths

convenience.restroom: Available at the ticket office, Stone Museum, and along all three courses
convenience.parking: Large free on-site parking lot

smartNotes.photo: Sky Pond (large reflecting pool — flagship shot); Mother's Room (interior sculpture casts a mother-and-child shadow when light hits it); Obaek Janggun (Five Hundred Generals) gallery for natural rock figures and basalt-rooted tree formations
smartNotes.facilities: Indoor Stone Museum and Seolmundae Halmang Exhibition Hall (climate-controlled); Obaek Janggun Gallery (multi-purpose art space); reconstructed Jeju thatched-roof village; cafe at the visitor center
smartNotes.tip: Allow 2–3 hours minimum (most tour buses only do Course 1). Last Wednesday of the month is FREE — busy. The walking is real — wear comfortable shoes. English signage is good throughout. The 300,000 pyeong (~99 ha) Gotjawal site is both park AND living lava-forest research area

_poi_meta.name_meaning: Seopji = narrow piece of land, Koji = Jeju dialect for 'cape'
_poi_meta.geological_feature: Bulgeun Oreum red volcanic cone with Bangdupo Lighthouse (octagonal, flashes every 4 sec)
_poi_meta.joseon_heritage: Hyeopja Yeondae Beacon Fire Station — 4m tall × 9m diameter volcanic-rock smoke signal mound (Special Monument No. 23-2)
_poi_meta.offshore_feature: Seonbawi/Seon Rock — 10m volcanic neck (dragon king's youngest son legend)

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor area accessible 24h; lighthouse stair access daylight hours; Glass House restaurant 10:00–21:00
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free area; parking ₩1,000 first 30 min, ₩500 each additional 15 min, max ₩3,000
visitBasics.walking: Easy — 2.1 km out-and-back paved coastal path with stairs up to the lighthouse (~14 m elevation); ~30 min walk; mostly flat with one steep stair section to the lighthouse

convenience.restroom: Available at the parking entrance and near the Glass House
convenience.parking: Large coastal parking lot (paid, max ₩3,000); fills quickly weekend mornings

smartNotes.photo: Bangdupo Lighthouse on Bulgeun Oreum for the cape's classic vista; Seonbawi (Standing Rock) offshore at low tide; All In Candy House (now abandoned but still photogenic); Glass House facade frames Seongsan Ilchulbong across the bay; spring canola fields (Mar–Apr) and June hydrangeas
smartNotes.facilities: Glass House restaurant (Tadao Ando design); Yumin Art Gallery (Tadao Ando); horse riding (extra cost); bakery cafe near Glass House; lighthouse trail; Joseon-era beacon fire station (Special Monument 23-2)
smartNotes.tip: Pair with Seongsan Ilchulbong (5.2 km / 10 min drive) for an optimal east-Jeju half-day. Best at sunset for the lighthouse silhouette + Seongsan view. Wear wind-resistant clothing — the cape catches strong sea winds year-round. Spring canola and summer hydrangeas are the two peak photo seasons

_poi_meta.cave_floor_treatment: Artificially leveled (vs Manjanggul's uneven natural floor) — accessible to most visitors
_poi_meta.cave_lighting: Decorative seven-color lighting (gold, silver, bronze, red, black, blue, white) along the open section
_poi_meta.alternate_to: Manjanggul Cave (functions as Jeju lava tube alternate when Manjanggul is closed for conservation/weather)
_poi_meta.drama_filming: Running Man (런닝맨) episode 89 (Alice in Wonderland theme, 2012)
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Cave proper is 365m of 1,700m (~25 min walk-through). The bulk of a typical 75-min stop is the surrounding outdoor park: Subyeon Park, sculpture street, bonsai garden, cactus greenhouse, traditional village mock-up, footbath cafe. 30% indoor / 70% outdoor.

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (last entry 1 hour before sunset); open 365 days a year
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Adult ₩12,000 / Youth ₩9,000 / Child ₩7,000; under 36 months free; foot-spa and special workshops priced separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat paved paths through the park; cave section ~365 m walk-through with low ceiling in places (clearance ~1.5 m in short sections); allow ~30 min through cave + 60–90 min for the rest of the park

convenience.restroom: Available at the entrance, mid-park, and near the cave
convenience.parking: Large free parking lot at the entrance

smartNotes.photo: Cave entrance with the colored-light interior; Subyeon Park waterfall; sculpture street figures; bonsai garden
smartNotes.facilities: Aroma footbath cafe (paid extra); reconstructed Jeju traditional village (with live black pig pens); cactus greenhouse; gift shop; Running Man fan signboards; Muslim prayer room
smartNotes.tip: When tour itineraries say 'Manjanggul alternate' or 'lava tube' on the east route, this is often Ilchul Land's Micheon Cave — easier walking than Manjanggul (leveled floor) and runs even when Manjanggul has closures. Bring a light jacket — the cave stays cool year-round (~10–13°C). Running Man fans should photograph the marked filming-location signboards

_poi_meta.designation: Important Folklore Cultural Heritage of South Korea (entire village + multiple individual buildings)
_poi_meta.natural_monuments: Two ancient trees (Saw-leaf Zelkova, Japanese Hackberry) at village center — designated Natural Monuments of South Korea
_poi_meta.historical_fires: Major fire 1826 (~80 buildings destroyed); fire 1948 during Jeju Uprising
_poi_meta.design_principle: Korean walled town (eupseong) on feng shui principles
_poi_meta.drama_filming: Dae Jang Geum (Jewel in the Palace, MBC 2003-2004)
_poi_meta.local_specialty_drink: Omegisul (rice-based traditional Jeju liquor, brewed by one family for multiple generations)
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Experience is dominated by walking the olle alleys, photographing the 600+ year Natural Monument trees (zelkova + hackberry) at the village center, and the stone-walled hanok exteriors — all outdoor. Hanok interior visits are 5-10 min each. 30% indoor / 70% outdoor.

visitBasics.hours: Outdoor village accessible during daylight; managed visitor center 09:00–18:00
visitBasics.closed: Living village — always open
visitBasics.admission: FREE entry to the village (it is a living community); some experience programs (hanbok rental, dyeing, archery) are paid separately
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat dirt and stone alleys (olle) inside the walled town; ~1 km loop covers the main heritage buildings

convenience.restroom: Available at the management office near the south gate
convenience.parking: Free parking outside the south gate

smartNotes.photo: South Gate (Namnan-mun) walls; ancient Saw-leaf Zelkova and Japanese Hackberry trees at the village center (Natural Monuments); thatched-roof hanok with characteristic Jeju black-stone walls; old administrative office (Igwanheon)
smartNotes.facilities: Management office offers FREE official guided tours (~30 min); experience programs include hanbok wearing, traditional dyeing, archery; restaurants serving Jeju black pork and traditional dishes; one family-run brewery serves omegisul (traditional rice liquor)
smartNotes.tip: ⚠ Important — only follow guides with the official management-office badge. Some private 'House to Visit' signs lead to high-pressure souvenir sales (cordyceps, organic soap, ginseng) at inflated prices. The Natural Monument trees (zelkova + hackberry) at the center are ~600+ years old and culturally protected. Filmed for MBC's Dae Jang Geum (Jewel in the Palace)

_poi_meta.seoubong_peak_status: Volcanic cone — climb takes ~1 hour to summit; spring canola fields on slopes
_poi_meta.wwii_history: Coastal cliffs hold tunnels carved by Imperial Japanese Army during occupation
_poi_meta.famous_cafe: Cafe Delmoondo (large beachfront cafe with terraces)

visitBasics.hours: Beach accessible 24h; designated swim period typically July 1–August 31 (lifeguarded)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: Free beach; free parking; some seasonal water-sport rentals are paid
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat sandy beach + paved coastal promenade; Seoubong Peak short hike adds steep but quick ~1 hr summit climb

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms, changing rooms and showers along the beach
convenience.parking: Large free parking lot (~300 cars) right at the beach; fills quickly summer weekends

smartNotes.photo: Mosaic seawall spelling 'Hamdeok' at the central beach plaza; black basalt rock-and-bridge isles linking the three coves; Seoubong Peak summit view back over the turquoise lagoon; the disappearing low-tide sandbar (forms each morning, vanishes by evening)
smartNotes.facilities: Cafe Delmoondo (most famous beachfront cafe), Bomnal Cafe, restaurants and convenience stores along Hamdeok-ro; free camping ground on the north side; water sports (kayak, SUP, surfing, banana boat, jet ski)
smartNotes.tip: Tide-dependent — at low tide the sea draws back and exposes a wide sandbar (one of Jeju's most distinctive beach features). Water is shallow and waist-high even far from shore — excellent for families with young children. Olle Trail Course 19 segment runs east via Seoubong Peak for a 1.5 hr coastal walk addition. WWII Japanese tunnels still visible in the eastern cliffs near Seoubong

_poi_meta.unesco_status: Culture of Jeju Haenyeo (women divers) — UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (inscribed 2016)
_poi_meta.first_written_record_source: 'Description of Jeju's Natural Features' and 'Collected Works of Guchang' by Lee Geon — recorded as 'Jamnyeo' (潛女)

visitBasics.hours: Tue–Sun 09:00–18:00 (last admission 17:00)
visitBasics.closed: Mondays, New Year's Day, Seollal, Chuseok; if Mon is a public holiday, closed the next business day
visitBasics.admission: Adult ₩1,100 / Youth (7–24) ₩500; group (10+) ₩800/₩300; FREE for under 7 and 65+
visitBasics.walking: Easy — fully indoor museum on 3 floors with elevators; outdoor garden flat. Stroller- and wheelchair-friendly throughout

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms inside the museum on each floor
convenience.parking: Free on-site parking

smartNotes.photo: Bronze haenyeo statues outside the entrance plaza; rooftop observatory deck overlooking the Hado-ri coast (where active haenyeo still dive); replica of a traditional haenyeo home interior (Hall 1)
smartNotes.facilities: 3 exhibition halls — Hall 1 (Haenyeo Diver's Life with home/meal restoration), Hall 2 (haenyeo work tools and history), Hall 3 (multimedia + UNESCO designation context); 1F gift shop with haenyeo-themed souvenirs; rooftop observatory; outdoor sculpture garden; English/Japanese/Chinese-supported audio + video presentations (10-min intro film available on request at front desk)
smartNotes.tip: Most exhibits have full English signage. Plan ~1 hour. The first written record of haenyeo dates to 1629 — they are a 400-year living tradition. After visiting, walk down to the Hado-ri coast (5 min) where active haenyeo still dive in the morning; or drive 5 min to Haenyeo restaurants in Gujwa for fresh shellfish straight from the divers' catch. UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage status was inscribed in 2016

_poi_meta.scale: Largest camellia arboretum in the East — 6,000 trees representing 500+ camellia species from 80 countries
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Predominantly outdoor garden walk (~85%); the only indoor refuges are the climate-controlled greenhouse (with hydrangea/camellia year-round display) and the gallery + cafe.

seasonal_blooms.autumn (Sep-Oct): Pink muhly grass + silver grass + osmanthus + early camellias

_poi_meta.drama_filming: Variety show 'EXchange / Transit Love 3' (환승연애) — Ju-won and Yu-jeong's memorable date scene

visitBasics.hours: Nov–Feb 08:30–18:00 / Mar–May & Sep–Nov 08:30–18:30 / Jun–Aug 08:30–19:00. Last admission 1 hour before closing. Open 365 days a year.
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round. NOTE: As there are no outdoor lamps on the grounds, admission may close earlier than scheduled on cloudy/short-daylight days or when sunset is early.
visitBasics.admission: Adult ₩12,000 / Teen ₩10,000 / Senior (65+) ₩10,000 / Child (3–12) ₩9,000 / under 3 free. Discount tickets on Trazy/Klook/Waug typically ₩7,000–8,500 adult.
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat paved garden paths through themed forests; full loop ~1.5–2 km, allow 1–2 hours; mostly stroller-friendly

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms across the garden
convenience.parking: Free on-site parking lot directly at the entrance

smartNotes.photo: Window-frame camellia shot (signature winter image — pink camellia branches framed in a small wooden window); glasshouse interior with white-rose-like camellias; pink muhly + Hallasan distance views in autumn; the tunnel-arch of red camellia fall in mid-winter
smartNotes.facilities: 5 cafes across the grounds (Flower Cafe, Hoobak Namu Cafe, Cafe & Gift Shop and others — drink package add-on at booking); art gallery with camellia-themed crafts; multipurpose seminar room; thatched-roof guest cottages and condominium-style resort lodging on-site; signature camellia-oil and beauty product shop
smartNotes.tip: Winter (Dec–Feb) is the iconic camellia season — book in advance on weekends. Even in summer, the garden stays beautiful with hydrangeas. Pets under 8kg allowed (verified via Klook). Allow 1.5–2 hours minimum — most guests overstay. Themed forests: European Camellia Forest + Asia Pacific Camellia Forest. Pair naturally with O'sulloc (10 min drive) for a half-day garden + tea route.

_poi_meta.address_en: San 1, Saekdal-dong, Seogwipo-si, Jeju-do (opposite the 1100 Highland Rest Area on Road 1100)
_poi_meta.ramsar_designation: Ramsar Wetland of International Importance — registered 12 October 2009 (Site #1893)
_poi_meta.within_unesco: Inside Hallasan UNESCO World Heritage Site + UNESCO Biosphere Reserve
_poi_meta.wetland_count: 16+ separate freshwater wetlands and pools spread discontinuously across the highland
_poi_meta.biodiversity: 3 threatened bird species + 1 threatened plant species (national designations); endemic mammals, reptiles, insects unique to Jeju
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Trail itself is 100% outdoor boardwalk. Only the 1100 Highland Rest Area opposite (convenience store / cafe) provides indoor refuge — typically 10-15 min stop.
_poi_meta.winter_status: Subject to road closure during heavy rain, snow, or icy conditions; tour itineraries typically substitute Jeju National Museum if Road 1100 is closed
_poi_meta.winter_appeal: Snow-covered boardwalk + frozen wetlands create a notable winter photography spot — bus 240 access maintained when possible

visitBasics.hours: Wetland trail accessible 24h (free); 1100 Highland Rest Area cafe/shop 09:00–17:00 typical
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round but road may close in heavy snow/rain; check Jeju Police Traffic Information before visit in winter
visitBasics.admission: FREE — Ramsar site, no entry fee, no parking fee
visitBasics.walking: Very easy — 1 km flat wooden boardwalk loop, 15–20 min walk; accessible to most mobility levels including with strollers (boardwalk surface)

convenience.restroom: Restrooms at the 1100 Highland Rest Area opposite the trailhead
convenience.parking: Free parking at the rest area (parking lot can be tight on snowy weekends — overflow on roadside)

smartNotes.photo: Wooden boardwalk over freshwater pools with Hallasan in the distance; winter snow scenes (one of Jeju's most photographed winter spots); white deer (Baekrok) statue at the rest area opposite (Jeju legend — immortals rode white deer to Hallasan summit)
smartNotes.facilities: 1100 Highland Rest Area opposite — convenience store (GS25), cafe with hot drinks/ramen, observatory, Go Sangdon Park honoring the first Korean to summit Everest, white deer (Baekrok) statue + monument, restrooms
smartNotes.tip: Most tour operators visit en route between Jeju City and Seogwipo via the south route. WINTER WARNING — Road 1100 is the first to close in heavy snow/rain/ice; private taxi tour drivers carry snow chains. Even in summer, temperatures at 1100m are 7–10°C cooler than coast — bring a light jacket. Bus 240 hourly from Jeju City. Observe migratory birds and wetland flora — interpretive signs along the boardwalk.

_poi_meta.alt_name: 99 Peaks (99봉) — multiple craters from secondary volcanic explosions inside the main crater
_poi_meta.trail_surface: Wooden boardwalk + paved sections + gentle stairs (well-maintained)
_poi_meta.trail_exposure: ~80% sun-exposed with no shade — bring sun protection
_poi_meta.main_crater_size: 500m diameter × 80m depth (cordoned off until July 2020 for habitat preservation)
_poi_meta.wwii_history: 17 Imperial Japanese Army cave trenches dug 1943-1945 along the coastal cliffs by forced Jeju resident labor (some sources cite 60+ tunnels in the broader cave-trench complex)
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: 100% outdoor coastal trail. Adjacent restaurants and cafes at the parking lot (Starbucks, seafood places) are off-trail.
_poi_meta.history_japanese_invasion: Used by Japanese army during late WWII invasion of China — Korea's most accessible WWII-era forced-labor heritage walk

visitBasics.hours: Trail accessible during daylight (no lighting); typically 06:00–sunset
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; some sub-trails (crater rim) restricted for habitat preservation
visitBasics.admission: FREE — no entry fee
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 2.8 km loop, ~108 m elevation gain, 1–1.5 hour typical pace; well-formed boardwalk with stairs in sections; easy enough for elementary-school children but exposed to sun and wind

convenience.restroom: Toilet facilities at the start and end of the circular trail (parking-lot area)
convenience.parking: Paid parking lot at trailhead — fills quickly weekend mornings; overflow parking on roadside near the entrance

smartNotes.photo: Coastal boardwalk with Sanbangsan + Hallasan in the same frame (one of Jeju's signature compositions); WWII Japanese-army cave trenches along the cliff (peer inside, no entry); Marado + Gapado island views; sunset over the southwest Pacific (the trail's highest-rated time)
smartNotes.facilities: Restaurants and cafes at the parking lot (Starbucks, seafood houses); horseback riding around an adjacent trail (extra cost); pine-forest section of the trail provides occasional shade
smartNotes.tip: Wear hat + sunscreen — 80% of the trail is exposed (very hot in summer). Best at sunset for photos — about 90 min before sunset start time. The 17 cave trenches along the coastal ridge are a visible WWII forced-labor memorial — Jeju residents were conscripted to dig them between 1943 and 1945. Olle Trail Course 10 passes through. Pair naturally with Sanbangsan Grotto + Yongmeori Coast (5 min drive) for a southwestern volcanic-coast morning.

_poi_meta.twin_beach_companion: Geumneung Beach (immediately south, less crowded — same shoreline, walkable)
_poi_meta.sand_origin: Crushed seashells + coral fragments mixed with fine sand — distinctive silvery-white color
_poi_meta.iconic_offshore: Biyangdo Island — 1.2 km offshore, volcanic island ~200 residents, 20-min ferry from Hallim Port; Korea's first carbon-free island certification process
_poi_meta.backdrop: Pine forest (Songlim) — free camping ground available behind the beach
_poi_meta.ranking: Top 10 Jeju beaches per most rankings; Top 3 west-coast Jeju beaches
_poi_meta.amenities: Public restrooms, changing rooms, showers, equipment rentals, life-guarded swim zones in summer, ramp-style wheelchair access
_poi_meta.tide_features: Low tide reveals tidal pools with hermit crabs, small shrimp, juvenile fish — shallow enough that Biyangdo 'appears walkable' at low tide
_poi_meta.famous_for: Sunset (sun setting behind Biyangdo Island over emerald water)
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Beach itself is 100% outdoor. The west-coast cafe strip behind the beach offers indoor refuges if needed — most tour stops are outdoor walks + photos.
_poi_meta.adjacent_attraction: Hallim Park — botanical garden + lava caves + folk village (separate ₩10,000 admission, 1.5 km away)

visitBasics.hours: Beach accessible 24h; nighttime opening also implemented in recent summers (2024+)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.admission: FREE — no entry fee; paid parking ~₩3,000–4,000/visit
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat sandy beach + paved promenade; wheelchair-accessible ramps from the parking lot

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms with foot-washing facilities at the beach entry
convenience.parking: Multiple paid parking lots fronting the beach (~₩3,000–4,000); fills quickly summer weekends

smartNotes.photo: Emerald lagoon + Biyangdo Island silhouette (signature shot — best from the east end of the beach); sunset over Biyangdo (Jeju's most-photographed west-coast sunset); black volcanic rock + white shell sand contrast at low tide; haenyeo bronze statue on the seawall promenade
smartNotes.facilities: West-coast cafe strip — Bonte Museum, Anthracite Coffee Hyeopjae, Cafe Wandermood and many others along Hyeopjae-ri shoreline (the beach is one of Jeju's top cafe-stop areas); equipment rentals (umbrellas ₩10,000, snorkel gear); Hallim Park 1.5 km away (separate ticket); free pine-forest camping ground (Songlim) directly behind the beach
smartNotes.tip: Twin-beach tip — walk 10 min south to Geumneung Beach for fewer crowds and same emerald water. Biyangdo Island ferries from Hallim Port (20 min, ~₩7,000 round-trip). Low tide is the magical time — tidal pools form between the lava rocks, perfect for kids exploring marine life. Sunset is the iconic photo moment. Pair with Hallim Park for a half-day west-coast loop. Late afternoon parking is easier than midday.

_poi_meta.festival_name: Jeju King Cherry Blossom Festival (제주 왕벚꽃축제)
_poi_meta.festival_period: Late March to early April (3-day window typical, dates vary year by year)
_poi_meta.trees_significance: The oldest tree-lined street of Yoshino (King) cherry trees in Jeju City — some trees over 100 years old at the start of the road
festival_features: 1.2 km section closed to vehicles (pedestrian-only) during festival
Night lighting installed around the trees — strolls and photography after dark
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Entirely outdoor street walk. Side cafes and restaurants nearby provide indoor refuge if needed.
_poi_meta.best_photo_time: Morning (7am) or 1-2 PM for peak sun direction; night for lit cherry tunnel

visitBasics.hours: Public street — accessible 24h. Festival hours and stage events typically 10:00–22:00 during the festival window.
visitBasics.closed: Always open as a city street; festival is annual late March / early April
visitBasics.admission: FREE — public street; festival admission free
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat 1.2 km festival section; suitable for strollers and seniors. Most visitors do a 30–60 min walk.

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms in nearby Jeju City Sports Complex and adjacent commercial buildings
convenience.parking: City parking lots nearby; festival days bring road closures — many guests use Jeju City buses or taxi

smartNotes.photo: The cherry-blossom tunnel along the 1.2 km festival corridor (peak morning light); night-illuminated trees with lanterns; close-ups of century-old Yoshino trees at the road's start
smartNotes.facilities: Cafes, Korean kalguksu/sujebi restaurants (e.g., A-bang at 9,000–12,000 KRW), tangerine-product stores along Jeonnong-ro and side alleys; festival food stalls during the bloom window
smartNotes.tip: Bloom window is short (typically 5–7 days) — check current-year festival dates before going. Tour itineraries that include this stop are season-dependent (late March / early April only). Night visits are particularly recommended — the lit cherry tunnel is uniquely photogenic. Drive from Jeju Airport: 10 minutes.

_poi_meta.name_en: Noksan-ro / Gasi-ri Cherry Blossom + Canola Road
_poi_meta.designation: Selected as one of the '100 Most Beautiful Roads in Korea'
_poi_meta.alt_name: First Sight of the Ten Sights of Gasiri Village
_poi_meta.historical_context: Passes through Noksanjang and Gammajang — Joseon Dynasty's premier horse-breeding farms
_poi_meta.associated_festival: Jeju Canola Flower Festival (early April; held at Gasi-ri Canola Plaza)
_poi_meta.uniqueness: Only road in Korea where King cherry blossoms (light pink) and canola flowers (golden yellow) bloom simultaneously over a 10 km stretch
additional_features: Wind power turbines along the route — modern landscape contrast
Ttarabi Oreum and Keunsaseum Oreum parasitic cones nearby
Jeju Horse Park (pony rides) at the Noksan-ro entrance
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: 100% outdoor — country road with photo stops along the route. Cafes in Pyoseon and the Jeju Horse Park visitor center offer occasional indoor refuge.
_poi_meta.peak_bloom_window: Late March to early April (cherry blossoms + canola simultaneously)

visitBasics.hours: Public country road — accessible 24h
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; cherry-blossom + canola only in late Mar–early Apr
visitBasics.admission: FREE — public road
visitBasics.walking: Mostly drive-and-stop — visitors pull over at scenic pull-outs every few hundred meters. Walking sections optional and flat.

convenience.restroom: Restrooms at Jeju Horse Park and Gasi-ri Canola Plaza; limited along the 10 km road itself
convenience.parking: Free roadside parking + designated photo-stop pull-outs; Canola Festival venue has organized parking

smartNotes.photo: Canola yellow + cherry blossom pink + wind turbines + blue sky — Jeju's most distinctive spring composition; horse + canola fields combination near Gasi-ri Horse Park; aerial views from Ttarabi Oreum
smartNotes.facilities: Jeju Horse Park (Jeju Horse Museum + Ttarabi horseback riding track + Maeum Cafe + Mongol Yurt Guesthouse + camping site at Gasi-ri); Blaze Resort pension on Noksan-ro; Saryeoni Forest Trail (separate attraction)
smartNotes.tip: Bloom window is even shorter than Jeonnong-ro (5–7 days, typically early April). Drive slowly with frequent stops — the yellow/pink/blue/turbine combination shifts every 500m. Combine with Jeonnong-ro on the same day for a complete cherry-blossom morning + countryside afternoon. Driving is the most practical access (Naver Maps key: 녹산로). Festival-period weekends bring traffic — early morning visits recommended.

_poi_meta.location_context: On the edge of Hallasan Mountain — backdrop visible throughout the park
_poi_meta.park_concept: Year-round flower park with rotating festivals — 'an island within an island'
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Predominantly outdoor flower gardens (~85%); indoor refuges include the hydrangea greenhouse, insect exhibit hall, gallery (Hueree Gallery Pang), and cafe.

annual_festival_calendar.Plum Blossom (Maehwa) Festival: February – late March (earliest plum festival in Korea)
annual_festival_calendar.Hydrangea Festival: March – August (long bloom season — primary identity)

permanent_features: Seasonal Flower Garden (13,223 sqm canola flower field per Trazy data)
Hot spring with high iron and mineral content (foot bath)
Petting zoo (piglets) + animal feeding (rabbits, black goats, ostriches, ducks, baby black pigs, horses)
_poi_meta.signature_show: Jeju Black Pig Show — 4 daily slots (11:00, 13:00, 15:00, 17:00); arrive 30 min early. Jeju Black Pig is a designated Natural Monument of Korea.
experiential: Traditional Korean games — building stone pagodas, throwing arrows, hoop spinning

visitBasics.hours: Mar–Sep 09:00–19:00 (last admission 17:30) / Oct–Mar 09:00–18:00 (last admission 16:30); Citrus picking last admission 16:00
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; check festival schedule for primary attraction in your visit window
visitBasics.admission: Adult ₩13,000 / Teen ₩11,000 / Child ₩10,000 / Senior 65+ ₩6,500; Group (20+) ₩9,000/₩8,000/₩7,000; under 25 months free. Citrus picking ₩5,000 extra; Horseback riding ₩10,000 extra.
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat paved garden paths; mostly stroller-friendly; allow 1.5–2.5 hours for full park

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms across the park
convenience.parking: Free on-site parking lot

smartNotes.photo: Hydrangea garden + Hallasan in the background (the park's signature shot, June–July peak); canola flower field with windmill backdrop (April peak); pink muhly grass swaying in autumn (Sep–Oct); plum blossom corridor (Feb–Mar); decade-old hanok in the cultural village
smartNotes.facilities: Cafe with green plum juice (signature drink); Jeju Cultural Village (traditional hanok displays); Hueree Gallery Pang (5-color hydrangea photo exhibitions during festival periods); insect exhibit; foot-bath hot spring; charcoal-ground barefoot walk; gift shop with plum and citrus products
smartNotes.tip: Most flexible flower park in Jeju — has SOME headliner festival running 11 of 12 months. **Hydrangea Festival (March–August) is the longest-running and most photogenic festival.** Confirm hydrangea greenhouse opening dates (it opens around mid-April for the spring festival). Allow 2 hours minimum. Black Pig Show is a kid-favorite — schedule around the 11:00/13:00/15:00/17:00 slots. Pet-friendly. Combine with Seongsan Ilchulbong (40 min drive) for an east-Jeju summer day.

_poi_meta.founded: 1971 by Song Bong-gyu — transformed a sandy wasteland into a green paradise
themed_areas: 3. Hyeopjaegul Cave + Ssangyonggul Cave (National Monument No. 236)
4. Jeju Ornamental Stone and Dwarfed Tree (Bonsai) Garden
5. Jae-am Folk Village (with tallest Stone Grandfather / Dolharubang)
_poi_meta.caves_significance: Hyeopjaegul (~200m × 10m × 5m, ~25 million years old, formed by Hallasan eruption) and Ssangyonggul (~400m × 6m × 3m, 'Two Dragons' branching paths) are part of a 17 km lava-tube system. Often described as 'the only two-dimensional caves in the world' — they began as lava tubes but were later coated with limestone (calcium carbonate) from shells in the soil above, creating the rare combination of lava-tube formations + limestone stalactites/stalagmites in a single cave system.
_poi_meta.caves_temperature_celsius: 17-18°C year-round (cool in summer, warm in winter)
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Two cave sections (combined ~600m) are fully indoor — natural temperature refuge in summer/winter. Folk village hanok interiors and Stone Exhibition Hall are partial-indoor. Approximately 80% outdoor gardens and trails.
famous_visitors: Former Hyundai Group Chairman Chung Ju-yung

visitBasics.hours: Mar-Sep 08:30-19:00 (last admission 17:30) / Oct-Feb 08:30-18:00 (last admission 17:00) / Peak summer Jul 15 – Aug 20: 08:30-19:30
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round
visitBasics.walking: Mostly easy — flat paved garden paths; cave sections have natural rock floors with ducking required in places. Allow 1.5–3 hours.

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms across the park including near caves and folk village
convenience.parking: Free on-site parking lot (large)

smartNotes.photo: Hyeopjaegul Cave entrance + lava-tube + limestone formations; Ssangyonggul cave's branching dragon paths; Palm Tree Avenue (signature shot); seasonal monthly festival flowers (hydrangea June, camellia Dec); Jae-am Folk Village with tallest Dolharubang; peacocks roaming the bird garden; Bonsai Garden with volcanic rock + miniature trees
smartNotes.facilities: Cafe, Jeju traditional restaurant in folk village, gift shop, children's amusement park, outdoor resort facilities. Buses #281 from Jeju Intercity Bus Terminal (50 min) or from Seogwipo (80 min) or Jungmun (60 min) all stop at Hallim Park.
smartNotes.tip: 11 of 12 months have a different headline flower festival — confirm which is active for your visit. June Hydrangea and December Camellia are top picks. Caves stay 17-18°C — bring a light layer in summer for the temperature shift, or enjoy the warmth in winter. Pet-friendly. Combine with Hyeopjae Beach (5-10 min walk) for a half-day west-coast pair.

_poi_meta.address_en: Eorimok Visitor Centre, Hallasan National Park, NW side; Bus 240 / 1100 stops at 1100 Altitude Rest Area
_poi_meta.trail_length: 4.7 km one-way to Witseoreum Shelter (commonly described); 7.7 km / 487 m elevation gain to viewpoint per Hunting for Bliss; 8.7 mi (14 km) out-and-back per AllTrails
_poi_meta.elevation_gain_m: 487-873 (varies by route segment); summit at Witse Oreum plateau ~1,700 m
_poi_meta.summit_destination: Witseoreum Shelter (1,700 m) — official turnaround; viewpoint detours visible nearby
_poi_meta.difficulty: Moderate (one of two easiest of 5 Hallasan trails) — does NOT reach summit
_poi_meta.time_roundtrip: 2.5 hours (per Fork and Foot) or 4-6 hours (per AllTrails / Hunting for Bliss for longer extension)
_poi_meta.reservation_note: No reservation required — unlike Seongpanak/Gwaneumsa summit routes which require advance booking. Eorimok is always available.
_poi_meta.entry_hours: Opens 05:00; last entry approximately 11:30 in winter, 12:30 in other seasons (varies by month — check current schedule)
trail_features: Long boardwalk sections protecting the volcanic ground
Dwarf bamboo fields at higher altitude
Forest section transitions to exposed plateau
Spectacular non-summit views of Hallasan summit wall on clear days
View over Jeju City + northern coastline from upper plateau
Witseoreum Shelter at turnaround with optional viewpoint detour
_poi_meta.winter_conditions: Crampons recommended November–March (basic strap-on crampons sold at trailheads ~$8 USD). Snow scenes on the mountain are dramatic; Korean hiking clubs plan last-minute trips after fresh snowfalls. Hoarfrost on dwarf trees creates a 'frozen guardian' landscape per published trekker accounts. Sudden weather changes possible — Jeju is semi-tropical and storms can arrive quickly even in winter.
alt_combination_routes: Eorimok ↑ + Yeongsil ↓ (point-to-point, 7 mi, 4.5-5 hrs, moderate) — most popular non-summit traverse
Eorimok ↑ + Donnaeko ↓ (longer non-summit traverse across the volcano)
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: 100% outdoor mountain trail. Visitor center at trailhead has minor indoor refuge (toilets, info). Witseoreum Shelter at turnaround is a small wooden enclosure but not a heated indoor space.
_poi_meta.parking: Free trailhead car park; fills on autumn weekends — arrive before 7AM on peak days
_poi_meta.bus_access: Bus 240 from Jeju Bus Terminal stops at 1100 Altitude Rest Area; Routes 1100 / 1100-1 (weekend variations) also serve this area

visitBasics.hours: 05:00 entry; last entry ~11:30 winter / ~12:30 other seasons (varies — check current month)
visitBasics.closed: Open year-round; weather closures possible during storms or heavy snow
visitBasics.admission: FREE — Hallasan National Park has no entry fee
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 4.7-7 km one-way depending on turnaround point; controlled gradient with long boardwalks. NOT senior-friendly for the full route, but the first 2 km is gentle and accessible. Crampons required Nov-Mar.

convenience.restroom: Visitor center at trailhead; outhouses near Witseoreum Shelter
convenience.parking: Free trailhead car park (limited capacity — fills on weekends)

smartNotes.photo: Snow-encrusted dwarf trees ('frozen guardians') in winter; the Witse Oreum plateau dropping toward Jeju City; Hallasan summit wall up-close; dwarf bamboo fields with snow tips; sea-of-clouds below the ridge
smartNotes.facilities: Visitor center (toilets, info); Witseoreum Shelter at turnaround (small wooden enclosure, no food); 1100 Altitude Rest Area (separate but nearby — has cafe + restrooms + parking); crampons sold at trailhead in winter ~$8 USD
smartNotes.tip: **Use as a 'Hallasan viewpoint' for tour itineraries — most tour groups don't hike the full 4.7 km but go 1-2 km in for the dramatic forest-to-plateau transition and views.** Far easier than Seongpanak/Gwaneumsa summit routes (which require advance reservation). No reservation required = always available. Winter is the most dramatic season but bring crampons. Drive from Jeju City: 25 min.

_poi_meta.experience_type: Generic experience — tour operators select among many farms across Jeju, primarily in Seogwipo / Namwon-eup / Jungmun area
_poi_meta.peak_season: November to February (winter); some farms operate year-round with greenhouse
_poi_meta.typical_fee_krw: ₩5,000-15,000 per person entry, often including 1kg take-home
standard_format: Farm provides basket and scissors (some provide gloves)
Eat-as-you-pick allowed (unlimited tasting on the farm)
Take-home tangerines weighed and charged per kg (or included in entry fee)
Friendly multilingual staff at most tourist-frequented farms

variety_calendar.Winter Peak (Dec-Jan): Noji + Hallabong (mid-Dec to mid-Jan is Hallabong peak)

citrus_varieties: Hallabong (한라봉) — most famous; juicy, sweet, easy-peel; mid-Dec to mid-Jan peak
Gamgyul (감귤) — large, sweet, perfect for juicing
Cheonhyehyang (천혜향) — large hybrid of tangerine + orange; sweet
Red Hyang (레드향) — juicy red-orange flesh; Hallabong + Noji blend

notable_farms.name: Gyulhyangi (귤향기) Experience Farm
notable_farms.near: Halla Arboretum (15 min walk from bus stop)
notable_farms.fee: ₩7,000 entry incl 1kg take-home; English-friendly staff; offers complimentary tangerine tea + biscuits + jam tasting

notable_farms.name: Bong Bong Tangerine Farm
notable_farms.fee: ₩5,000 entry + ₩2,500/kg; cafe upstairs; Hallabong window mid-Dec to mid-Jan; named after owner's golden retriever

notable_farms.name: Choenamdan Experience Tangerine Farm
notable_farms.feature: Monorail ride to orchards; offers Hallabong + Cheonhyehyang

notable_farms.name: Jeju Southernmost Theme Park
notable_farms.feature: GAP-certified; year-round picking (greenhouse for off-season); 1kg included; monorail; petting zoo (rabbits, deer, ducks, black pigs)

notable_farms.name: Jejue In Farm
notable_farms.feature: Cafe + orchard combo; Hallabong-ade signature drink; photo zones

_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Mostly outdoor orchards (~90%); rain backup with greenhouse picking at most farms. Some farms include indoor cafe + product sales area.
_poi_meta.historical_context: Jeju citrus has been gifted seasonally to Korean royalty since the Baekje period (4th–7th c) through Silla and Goryeo dynasties — the cultivation tradition is over 1,500 years old.
_poi_meta.rain_backup: Most farms have greenhouse sections used as rain backup

visitBasics.hours: Typical farm hours 09:00-18:00; last admission 17:00; year-round farms (Jeju Southernmost) operate every day except Sunday; smaller farms vary
visitBasics.closed: Most farms operate seasonally (Nov-Feb peak); year-round options available
visitBasics.admission: Typical ₩5,000-15,000 entry per person; usually includes 1kg take-home; eat-as-you-pick allowed
visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat orchard paths; family-friendly; suitable for most ages and stroller-accessible at most farms

convenience.restroom: Most farms have restrooms
convenience.parking: Free farm parking (small lots; reserve in peak season)

smartNotes.photo: Tangerine-laden tree branches at picking height; orange + green orchard rows; family with picking baskets; close-up of Hallabong with its distinctive bumpy crown; cafe-orchard combo shots at Jejue In or Jejugyul Bate
smartNotes.facilities: Farm-to-table cafes serve tangerine-ade, hot tangerine tea, tangerine jam toast, Hallabong cake; gift shop with tangerine jam, soap, candles, chocolate, juice (popular souvenir items)
smartNotes.tip: **Hallabong window is narrow — mid-December to mid-January for peak sweetness.** Earlier (Oct-Nov) means Noji tangerines only. Reservations recommended in peak season for popular farms (Gyulhyangi, Bong Bong). Most farms accept walk-ins. Bring layers — winter Jeju orchards can be cold + windy. Children under 3 cannot ride monorail at Choenamdan / Southernmost. Jeju Southernmost is the year-round + GAP-certified option for off-season tour itineraries.

berths: Main Berth — 360 m, 12 m water depth, 140,000-ton capacity (1 cruise ship max)
Emergency Berth — 306 m, 11.5 m water depth, 85,000-ton capacity
transit_options: Bus #100 (airport ↔ Jeju cruise terminal, via downtown / hotel cluster)
Free shuttle bus to Dongmun Traditional Market (~10 min, oldest/largest market in Jeju City)
Taxi base fare ₩3,800; airport ~15 min by taxi
K-ride / Kakao T apps recommended for English-language taxi booking
On-foot: 25-min walk to Jeju City center (mostly flat)
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Logistic node — pickup/dropoff stop only; ~30 min total spent inside terminal building or in adjacent bus parking area (counted as indoor for time-weighting since the meaningful activity is meeting the guide and boarding the vehicle).
_poi_meta.history: The Port of Jeju (제주항) was made a seaport around 1913; the modern Jeju International Passenger Terminal (cruise + ferry) opened in 2015 to receive international cruise ships. South Korea's Ministry of Oceans & Fisheries announced in August 2019 a third dedicated cruise harbor on Jeju's northern coast (areas: Samdo-dong, Geonip-dong, Yongdam-dong) — KRW 2.8 trillion budget, scheduled completion 2040.
_poi_meta.korea_2024_cruise_volume: South Korea welcomed 810,000 cruise tourists aboard 414 ships in 2024 (Ministry of Culture, Sports and Tourism) — nearly tripling from 273,000 the prior year.
nearby_attractions: Dongmun Traditional Market (10-min drive, oldest in Jeju City)
Jeju City old downtown (~25 min walk)
Yongduam Rock / Dragon Head Rock (~5 min drive)
Samseonghyeol (Caves of the Three Clans) — legendary origin site of Jeju's three founding clans

visitBasics.hours: Cruise terminal operates per ship arrival schedule; airport-style facilities open during ship-day operations
visitBasics.closed: No fixed closure — operates by ship schedule
visitBasics.admission: Free for transit; cruise passengers cleared via ship's standard immigration
visitBasics.walking: Minimal — flat terminal interior; long walkway with moving sidewalks + elevators for mobility-challenged passengers

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms in arrival/departure halls
convenience.parking: Bus parking lot (capacity 100) + on-site car park; free shuttle drop-off zone

smartNotes.photo: Terminal exterior on cruise-ship arrival; traditional folklore performers occasionally welcome arriving passengers (varies by ship); harbor view of cruise vessels alongside ferries
smartNotes.facilities: Convenience store, pharmacy, snack shop, souvenir shops with tax-free items, Jeju Bank ATM accepting overseas cards, currency exchange (typical hours 08:00–17:00), SK Telecom T-mobile Wi-Fi rental counter, tourist info center (can have 30-min wait at peak)
smartNotes.tip: Allow 30+ min between ship debarkation and bus boarding — customs/immigration line at the northern terminal can swamp during multi-ship arrivals. Free shuttle to Dongmun Market is the fastest no-cost option for guests not on a tour. Taxis sometimes try long routes — state destination clearly and photograph the dashboard license. Bus #100 connects directly to the airport for guests with same-day evening flights from Jeju. At sailaway, plan to be back at terminal 60 min before all-aboard time.

_poi_meta.official_korean_name: Jeju Civilian-Military Complex Port for Beautiful Tourism (제주 민군복합형관광미항)
berths: Western Seawall (Berths 11-12) — 420 m, 12-15 m water depth, 220,000-ton capacity
Southern Seawall (Berths 21-22) — 690 m, 17 m water depth, 220,000-ton capacity
transit_options: Public bus connection to Seogwipo Bus Terminal (~10 min)
Limousine bus 800 / 800-1 (Jeju Airport ↔ Seogwipo via Innovation City — driver must be informed of destination before tap)
Taxi to Jungmun Resort Complex / Seogwipo downtown (~15 min)
_poi_meta.indoor_outdoor_note: Logistic node — pickup/dropoff stop only; ~30 min total inside terminal or adjacent bus area.
_poi_meta.history: Construction started 2007 as a joint civilian-military project. Plan first announced 1993 (Kim Young Sam presidency); Gangjeong selected May 2007 over Hwasun and Wimi candidate sites. Construction halted 7 times by environmental + anti-militarization protests (2011 referendum: 94% of Gangjeong's eligible voters opposed). South Korean Supreme Court upheld construction July 2012. The Civilian-Military Complex Port for Beautiful Tourism opened February 2016; cruise passenger operations began 2017.
